parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Describe two ways in which human activities can degrade soil quality.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for each correctly described way, up to <marks>4 marks</marks>:

- Intensive agriculture: Depleting nutrients and organic matter
- Deforestation: Increasing erosion and altering soil structure
- Urbanization: Sealing soil surface and altering hydrology
- Pollution: Contaminating soil with chemicals or heavy metals

parts.content: Explain the concept of soil compaction and its effects on soil health.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant answer, <marks>3 marks max</marks>:

- Soil compaction is the compression of soil particles, reducing pore space <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It decreases water infiltration and air movement in soil <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It limits root growth and nutrient uptake by plants <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil compaction can be caused by heavy machinery or overgrazing <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1G Soil properties (HL)

parts.content: Outline four differences between soils rich in sand and soils rich in clay.
parts.markscheme:
- Sandy soils have larger particle size / larger pore spaces; clay soils have smaller particle size / smaller pore spaces; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have higher permeability / drain more quickly; clay soils have lower permeability / drain slowly and are prone to waterlogging; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have lower water-holding capacity; clay soils have higher water-holding capacity; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have lower nutrient retention / lower cation exchange capacity; clay soils have higher nutrient retention / higher cation exchange capacity due to greater surface area; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between management strategies that could improve agricultural productivity on sandy soils and those on clay-rich soils. Include a supported conclusion about which soil type is generally easier to manage for crop production.
parts.markscheme:
- Sandy soils: add organic matter (compost/manure) to increase water retention and nutrient-holding capacity; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils: apply fertilizers in smaller, more frequent doses to reduce leaching losses; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils: use mulching/ground cover to reduce evaporation and improve soil structure; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: improve drainage (e.g., drainage channels/tiles, raised beds) to reduce waterlogging; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: avoid cultivation when wet / reduce compaction (controlled traffic, appropriate timing) to maintain aeration and structure; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: incorporate organic matter/gypsum (where appropriate) to improve aggregation and workability; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion: e.g., sandy soils often easier to work and drain but need more inputs for fertility/water, whereas clay soils retain nutrients/water but can be harder to cultivate due to compaction/waterlogging; clear judgement supported by preceding points; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which soil texture (sand versus clay content) determines the sustainability of food production in an agricultural system.
parts.markscheme:
0 marks: The response is not relevant to the question or no standard reached.

1–3 marks: Limited statements about sandy or clay soils with minimal use of ESS terminology; little or no evaluation of sustainability; few links to food production; no or unclear conclusion.

4–6 marks: Some accurate knowledge of how texture affects water and nutrient cycling, erosion risk, and management inputs; some evaluation of sustainability (e.g., trade-offs between irrigation/fertiliser needs, runoff/waterlogging, greenhouse gas implications); includes at least one relevant example; conclusion present but limited support.

7–9 marks: Thorough, balanced evaluation showing how texture influences productivity and environmental impacts (e.g., leaching/eutrophication risk in sandy soils, runoff/erosion and waterlogging risks in clay soils, implications for irrigation, fertiliser use, and soil conservation); integrates multiple perspectives/HL lenses (ecological, economic, cultural/political) and shows clear links between points; uses well-explained examples; explicit, well-supported conclusion addressing the extent to which texture determines sustainability relative to other factors (e.g., climate, organic matter, management practices).

parts.content: Explain the processes involved in the formation of fertile soils from bare rock.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following, <marks>4 mark max</marks>

- (mechanical/chemical) weathering/breaking up of parent rock producing small particles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- deposition of sediment/eroded material (through wind/water) increasing soil depth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- dissolving of particle material to release soluble minerals
- colonization of parental rock/sediments by plants/animals/decomposers/pioneer species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- biological/atmospheric nitrogen fixation adds available nitrogen (nitrates, ammonium) to soil <marks>1 mark</marks>
- activity of mycorrhizal fungi/decomposition of dead organisms/leaf litter to release mineral/nutrients/organic material/humus <marks>1 mark</marks>
- growth of mosses form mats that stabilize soils in sterile/barren ecosystems v
- earthworms/burrowing insects spread soil particles/open soil pores <marks>1 mark</marks>
- precipitation adding water to soil <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how negative and positive feedback mechanisms may influence the growth of decomposer populations in the soil.
parts.markscheme:
### Negative Feedback

- Negative feedback occurs when the output of a process inhibits or reverses the same process / in such a way as to reduce change/counteract deviation/maintain equilibrium.

- Growth of decomposer populations will reduce available organic material <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Thus leading to reduced growth in population <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Growth of decomposer population may lead to increase in predators feeding on decomposers <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Thus leading to reduction in population growth <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Positive Feedback 

- Positive feedback occurs when the output of a process accelerates that same process (will tend to amplify changes) / deviate away from equilibrium / or drive the system toward a tipping point (adopting a new equilibrium).

- Increase in number of decomposers will increase potential for reproduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Thus leading to increased rate of population growth <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Increase in decomposers may favorably modify environment (e.g. increase temperature/improve soil) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Thus leading to increased rate of population growth <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates may answer with the help of a diagram (e.g. feedback-links diagram) for which credit should be given.</note>
<note>Award [2] max for each valid feedback loop correctly identified.</note>  
<note>Only give credit if a complete loop is identified i.e. an increase of factor X leading eventually to a decrease or further increase of factor X (or the converse).</note>
<note>Do not credit simple changes in decomposer populations.</note>

<marks>7 marks max</marks>

parts.content: To what extent are natural limiting factors more likely than population policies to limit global human population growth in the future?
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

- Limited food supply due to constraints on arable land, water resources, and impacts of climate change 
- Lack of access to clean water and increased water stress 
- Spread of diseases facilitated by high population densities and poor sanitation 
- Environmental degradation reducing carrying capacity (deforestation, desertification, pollution) 
- Family planning programs and education to reduce fertility rates 
- Economic incentives/disincentives to influence reproductive choices 
- Improving access to contraception and reproductive health services 
- Policies promoting gender equality and women's empowerment 

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| <marks>0</marks> | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| <marks>1-3</marks> | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| <marks>4-6</marks> | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| <marks>7-9</marks> | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |


<note> Candidates should consider the extent to which natural factors (resource constraints, environmental degradation) and human policies (family planning, economic incentives) may limit population growth, providing a balanced analysis with examples. A well-supported conclusion is expected.</note>

parts.content:
Solid domestic waste may contain non-biodegradable material and toxins that have the potential to reduce the fertility of soils.

Explain how strategies for the management of this waste may help to preserve soil fertility.

The provision of food resources and assimilation of wastes are two key factors of the environment that determine its carrying capacity for a given species.

To what extent does the human production of food and waste each influence the carrying capacity for human populations?
parts.markscheme:
_The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although “ESS terminology” has been conflated with “Understanding concepts”). This guide simply provides some **possible** inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band._

_Answers may include_:

*   **understanding concepts and terminology** of carrying capacity, ecological footprint, waste assimilation, recycling/re-use/reduction strategies, BOD, toxicity, greenhouse gases, global warming/climate change, selective breeding/genetic engineering, commercial vs artisanal farming, mono-cropping, soil quality, desertification, Malthus/Boserup theories, _etc_
*   **breadth in addressing and linking** a range of ways in which different sources of waste _eg_ agricultural, domestic, industrial, transport _etc_ and food production systems _eg_ commercial, polyculture, artisanal, etc impact/deplete available resources thus influencing carrying capacity for humans, _etc_
*   **examples** of how carrying capacity may be influenced through wastes _eg_ toxicity to natural populations, increasing BOD in aquatic systems, greenhouse gases leading to global warming/climate change reducing (or possibly increasing?) productivity, and through food production _eg_ use of pesticides reducing natural populations, reducing soil quality through overharvesting/compaction/irrigation, habitat destruction, _etc_ and examples of how these influences may be reduced through waste management strategies _eg_ recycling, _etc_, alternative energy, and alternative agricultural practices _eg_ polyculture, crop rotation, more efficient crops/GMOs, _etc_
*   **balanced analysis** of the net impact of waste and food production on the carrying capacity for humans etc
*   **a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given** _eg_ “Although there are many mitigating strategies that can reduce the influence of waste and food production on carrying capacity, overall, an increasing per capita footprint of humans will inevitably result in a reduced carrying capacity.”

_Refer to paper 2 markbands, available under the 'your tests' tab > supplemental materials._

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1F Soil formation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1F Soil formation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks about the process of soil formation and how it is influenced by climatic factors, which is the direct focus of topic 5.1F.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which climate affects soil formation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Climate affects the rate of weathering of rocks and minerals, which is a key process in soil formation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Warmer and wetter climates increase the rate of chemical weathering, leading to faster soil formation
  - Colder and drier climates slow down chemical weathering, resulting in slower soil formation

- Climate influences the type of vegetation that grows in an area <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Vegetation contributes organic matter to the soil through leaf litter and dead roots
  - Different vegetation types produce different amounts and types of organic matter, affecting soil formation

### Method #2

- Precipitation levels in a climate affect the rate of leaching (removal of soluble minerals) from the soil <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - High rainfall leads to increased leaching, resulting in nutrient-poor soils
  - Low rainfall leads to less leaching, allowing minerals to accumulate in the soil

- Temperature and moisture levels in a climate determine the activity of soil organisms <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Warm and moist conditions promote the growth of soil organisms, which break down organic matter and contribute to soil formation
  - Cold and dry conditions inhibit the activity of soil organisms, slowing down soil formation

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the environmental and socio-economic impacts of the Green Revolution.
parts.markscheme:
### Environmental Impacts

- Biodiversity loss through widespread adoption of monocultures, as only high-yield crop varieties were prioritized <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Soil degradation resulted from intensive use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Increased irrigation demands led to groundwater depletion in many agricultural regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Socio-Economic Impacts

- Significant increase in food production and crop yields helped feed growing populations <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Created economic disparities as wealthier farmers benefited more than small-scale farmers <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Agricultural mechanization reduced labor requirements, affecting rural employment opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>6 marks max</marks>

This topic covers how soil functions as a medium for plant growth, a habitat for organisms, and a system for water purification and nutrient cycling.

parts.content: Describe two of the primary functions of soil.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant answer, <marks>2 marks max</marks>: 

  - Soil stores and purifies water
  - Soil provides habitat for living organisms (bacteria/worms/moles)
  - Soil modifies atmosphere through respiration of soil organisms and plant roots

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1E Soil horizons (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1E Soil horizons (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about transfers and transformations between the O, A, B, and C soil horizons, which directly assesses understanding of soil horizon structure and processes between horizons.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Discussing transfers and transformations between horizons also involves the soil as a system with flows (inputs/outputs, movement of water, nutrients, organic matter) through interconnected components.

parts.content: Outline the transfers and transformations between the O, A, B, and C soil horizons.
parts.markscheme:
- Decomposition of organic matter (e.g. leaves) in O horizon by decomposers and detritivores breaking down matter into plant nutrients <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Transfer of decomposed organic matter (DOM) from O horizon to A horizon through:
  - Movement by organisms (e.g. earthworms) between O-A horizons
  - Deposition of matter in different horizon locations <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Water movement and mineral transport:
  - Downward water movement until reaching impermeable layer (usually B horizon)
  - Leaching of soluble materials (minerals/nutrients) from A horizon to E horizon
  - Capillary action moving water upward from C to A/O horizon due to evaporation <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Nutrient cycling:
  - Plant absorption of minerals from A/O horizon
  - Nitrate leaching from A to B horizon
  - Denitrification releasing nitrates from O horizon to atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 2 marks each for any two of the above points</note>

questions.specification:
The transfer of energy and matter through trophic levels determines the productivity of an ecosystem.

**Table 1: Productivity data for a grassland ecosystem**

| Parameter | Energy ($$MJ \cdot m^{-2} \cdot yr^{-1}$$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) | 85.0 |
| Respiration (R) | 48.0 |

**Figure 1: Simplified terrestrial carbon cycle**

![Carbon Cycle](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-76f4e677-53eb-4ad5-8498-a36923b28dfb-1771929956947/c6a72fe0-144e-438a-8fae-1ee2f8bec21f.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5C Ecosystem resilience
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5C Ecosystem resilience
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) examines human roles in destabilizing ecological systems, closely linked to ecosystem resilience, disturbance, and loss of stability.

parts.content: Explain how soil can be viewed as an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
Like an ecosystem, soil is an open system with inputs and outputs:
- (inputs of) _e.g._ water/$O_2$ (and outputs of) _e.g._ $CO_2$/nitrogen <marks>1 mark</marks>

...and storages and flows/processes:
- (storages of) _e.g._ nitrates/water (and flows of) _e.g._ leaching/decomposition <marks>1 mark</marks>

Like an ecosystem, soil is a community of living/biotic and abiotic elements:
- (biotic) _e.g._ bacteria/fungi/earthworms (and abiotic) _e.g._ clay/sand/silt/water/heat <marks>1 mark</marks>

...with the many complex interactions/interrelationships/_e.g._ mineral cycling/leguminous plants <marks>1 mark</marks>

Interacts with/supports other systems/_e.g._ vegetation growth, animal movement/burrowing/human development <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>4 max</marks>


<note>Award marks as above for identifying relevant common features, and also for giving examples (provided it is clear what is being exemplified).</note>

parts.content: Compare and contrast the impact of humans on the carbon and nitrogen cycles.
parts.markscheme:
- In both cycles, combustion (of forests/fossil fuels) increases concentration of oxides in the atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In both cycles, deforestation/agriculture/sewage discharge leads to decomposition that also releases oxides <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both oxides (carbon dioxide and nitrous oxides) will increase the impact of global warming/climate change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both oxides result in the acidification of water/aquatic bodies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Deforestation removes organic storages of both nitrogen and carbon (stored in plant biomass) <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Contrast the impact of humans on the carbon and nitrogen cycles

- Carbon dioxide released (by respiration) into the atmosphere, whereas nitrous oxides are released into soil water (by nitrification) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Nitrous oxides contribute to a smaller degree to global warming compared to carbon dioxide <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Only nitrous oxides may cause acid deposition/acidify soils <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Deforestation reduces absorption of carbon from the atmosphere (via photosynthesis), but not nitrogen <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Deforestation causes soil erosion which reduces inorganic nitrogen storages in soil, but not carbon <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of inorganic fertilizers increases nitrogen in soil, but not carbon <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Run-off from fertilizers may cause excessive inorganic nitrogen in aquatic systems, but not carbon <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pesticide/herbicide use in agriculture might kill organisms, thus reducing both carbon and nitrogen organic storages (stored in their biomass) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - But the effect on the carbon cycle is limited to reducing respiration by soil animals, whereas it reduces nitrification/denitrification/decomposition processes for nitrogen <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extraction of oil/coal/gas reduces underground (ancient) carbon storages/transfers carbon storages to the surface (for human use), whereas the effect on the nitrogen cycle is limited to a few organic compounds/aromatics found in oil <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>7 max</marks>

<note>Award 5 max if only similarities or only differences are identified.</note>

parts.content: Examine the role of humans in the destabilization of ecological systems.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

* **Understanding concepts and terminology** of steady state and dynamic equilibria, stability, resilience, tipping-points, diversity, storage size, productivity, negative/positive feedback, complexity, community interdependence/interaction, human impacts of overexploitation/unsustainable harvesting, pollution, habitat degradation/destruction, unsustainable development, _etc_

* **Breadth in addressing and linking** factors that provide stability/resilience _e.g._ storage size, diversity, productivity, complexity, _etc_ with human activities that either weaken/reduce them _e.g._ overfishing, intensive agriculture, C emissions/global warming, water/atmospheric pollution, deforestation, urbanisation, mining/resource extraction, _etc_ or activities that strengthen/protect them _e.g._ legislation setting pollution limits/standards/fishing quotas, sustainable development schemes, alternative/renewable energies, conservation efforts/reserves, _etc_

* **Examples** of specific negative impacts on stability of ecological systems _e.g._ overfishing reduces the storage size of targeted fish populations reducing their stability, C emissions lead to climate change that reduces primary productivity of systems, eutrophication will interfere with negative feedback loops in freshwater systems, atmospheric pollutants may eliminate sensitive species of lichen reducing diversity, _etc_ and positive impacts on stability _e.g._ in-situ conservation protects complexity of relationships in system, agricultural techniques conserving soils maintain high productivity, _etc_

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) of the question specifically asks the student to explain soil as an open system involving the exchange of matter and energy, which is a core component of the 'Soil system' topic.

parts.content: Identify the soil horizon characterized as the "zone of illuviation," where minerals and nutrients leached from above accumulate.
parts.markscheme: B horizon (or subsoil) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Soil is considered a dynamic, open system. Explain three ways in which the soil system interacts with its surroundings through the exchange of matter or energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Soil receives energy inputs in the form of solar radiation, which regulates temperature and drives biological activity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It gains matter through inputs like precipitation (water) and organic debris (leaf litter) from the biosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil loses matter to other systems through outputs such as the leaching of minerals into groundwater or the loss of topsoil through wind/water erosion <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>3 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Describe three characteristics that distinguish the A-horizon from the B-horizon in a well-developed soil profile.
parts.markscheme:
- Color: The A-horizon is typically darker than the B-horizon due to a higher concentration of decomposed organic matter (humus) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biological Activity: The A-horizon has significantly higher levels of biological activity (e.g., earthworms, bacteria, plant roots) compared to the B-horizon <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Translocation process: The A-horizon is the zone of eluviation (where materials are washed out), whereas the B-horizon is the zone of illuviation (where materials accumulate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Composition: The A-horizon consists of mineral particles mixed with organic matter, while the B-horizon is primarily inorganic, composed of accumulated clays, iron, or aluminum oxides <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each distinct difference described, up to a maximum of 3 marks.</note>

<marks>3 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain the concept of soil horizons and describe the characteristics of three main horizons.
parts.markscheme:
Soil horizons are distinct layers in the soil profile.

<marks>1 mark</marks>

A horizon: Topsoil, rich in organic matter

<marks>1 mark</marks>

B horizon: Subsoil, accumulation of minerals and clay particles

<marks>1 mark</marks>

C horizon: Parent material, partially weathered rock

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two methods for measuring soil organic matter content.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Combustion method / loss on ignition
  - Dry soil sample is weighed
  - Sample is heated to high temperature (around 500°C) to burn off organic matter
  - Sample is reweighed after heating
  - Mass loss is due to organic matter being burned off
  <marks>2 marks</marks>

### Method #2

- Oxidation method
  - Soil sample is treated with an oxidizing agent like potassium dichromate / hydrogen peroxide
  - Organic matter is oxidized / broken down
  - Remaining solution is titrated / analyzed to determine amount of oxidizing agent used
  - Amount of oxidizing agent used is proportional to organic matter content
  <marks>2 marks</marks>

### Method #2

- Burrowing/tunnelling/bioturbation $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Mixing of soil layers/horizons $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
